metadata.title: Daiva Jankus | Sworn Lithuanian Interpreter & Professional Legal Guardian | Mönchengladbach
metadata.description: Sworn Lithuanian–German interpreter (OLG Düsseldorf) and registered professional legal guardian in Mönchengladbach. Two mandates, one understanding of responsibility.

hero.title: Legally binding mediation — in language and in life.
hero.lead: Sworn Lithuanian–German interpreter and registered professional legal guardian in Mönchengladbach. Two mandates, one understanding of responsibility — for courts, public authorities, businesses and families.

mandates.title: Two practices, one understanding of responsibility.
mandates.lede: Both mandates share the same DNA — state-authorised, personally accountable, with no machine in between.

i.title: Interpreting & Translation
i.desc: Court, authority and notarial interpreting; certified translations of all official documents. Lithuanian–German, in both directions.

ii.title: Legal Guardianship
ii.desc: Professional legal guardianship focused on financial care, healthcare and residence matters. Personal representation of adults who can no longer manage their affairs themselves.

intersection.title: When the law speaks another language.
intersection.body: Tens of thousands of people in Germany have Lithuanian as their first language — many of them now in advanced age, in care facilities, or in complex legal situations. When the family is far away and the counterpart speaks the formal German of authorities, a gap opens that translation alone cannot close. The combination of legal guardianship with native-language fluency is rare in Germany — and often exactly what family courts, social services, care facilities and families with Lithuanian-speaking relatives actually need.

cases.title: Lithuanian-speaking seniors in German care facilities
cases.body: The language of childhood returns with age, while care is delivered in German. A guardian fluent in both worlds closes the gap.

cases.title: Families with care needs and language barriers
cases.body: When relatives live in Lithuania and decisions fall in Germany, an on-site presence is needed who explains in both directions.

cases.title: Court-appointed guardianship with cultural and linguistic proximity
cases.body: Guardianship courts increasingly seek persons close to the affected individual in language and culture — a suitability that must be documented.

differentiation.title: Why a person — and not a system.

items.title: Legal admissibility
items.body: Certified translation, court interpreting and legal guardianship in Germany are tied to personal authorisation and registration. No algorithm can step in here.

items.title: Confidentiality & responsibility
items.body: Professional duty of confidentiality, personal handling of sensitive material, liability under one's own name. In guardianship: trusteeship over decisions about a life.

items.title: Presence & judgement
items.body: Negotiations, care conversations and authority appointments live on tone, context and human judgement. Both mandates require attendance — not retrieval.

aboutTeaser.title: Three decades between two languages — and two responsibilities.
aboutTeaser.body: Born in Lithuania, working in Germany since 1995. Generally sworn interpreter (OLG Düsseldorf) and authorised translator for Lithuanian–German. Since 2023 also a registered professional legal guardian under the BtOG, with documented qualification and a practice based in Mönchengladbach. Active in courts, business and the personal representation of adults — in German and Lithuanian.
aboutTeaser.photoAlt: Photo of Daiva at her desk

items.q: Who is allowed to interpret Lithuanian in German courts?
items.a: In German courts, only generally sworn interpreters may act in this capacity (§ 189 GVG). Daiva Jankus is generally sworn for the Lithuanian language by the Higher Regional Court of Düsseldorf and may be engaged anywhere in Germany.

items.q: What is legal guardianship in Germany?
items.a: Legal guardianship is ordered by the guardianship court for adults who can no longer manage their affairs in whole or in part. It replaced the former tutelage system and is oriented toward necessity and the welfare of the person under care (§§ 1814 et seq. BGB).

items.q: Can I propose Daiva Jankus as guardian?
items.a: Yes. When ordering a guardianship, those affected or their relatives may propose a particular person to the guardianship court. The court is required to follow this proposal as a rule, unless there are reasons against it (§ 1816 BGB).

items.q: Where does Daiva Jankus work?
items.a: Mainly in North Rhine-Westphalia — Düsseldorf, Cologne, Mönchengladbach, Essen, Dortmund — and elsewhere in Germany by arrangement. For longer engagements, travel outside NRW is included.

dolmetschen.metaTitle: Lithuanian–German Interpreter | Sworn OLG Düsseldorf | Daiva Jankus
dolmetschen.metaDescription: Sworn court interpreter and authorised translator Lithuanian–German in NRW. Authorised by the OLG Düsseldorf. Court interpreting, certified translations, business engagements.

hero.title: Interpreting & translation Lithuanian–German.
hero.lead: Generally sworn interpreter and authorised translator for the Lithuanian language, admitted by the Düsseldorf Higher Regional Court. Active for over thirty years across courts, business and the personal sphere — in North Rhine-Westphalia and across Germany.

services.title: Three fields, one standard.
services.lede: Each engagement asks for a different kind of precision. What remains constant is the legal weight of every word, spoken or written.

items.title: Courts & Authorities
items.desc: Court interpreting, notarial appointments and proceedings before public authorities. Certified translations of case files and official documents — recognised across Germany.
bullets: Notary appointments, attestations, powers of attorney

items.title: Business
items.desc: Negotiation-grade interpreting for contract closings, plant visits and sensitive compliance matters in German–Lithuanian commerce.

items.title: Private clients
items.desc: Personal occasions in which language has legal consequences — from civil weddings to estate matters, from diplomas to medical accompaniment.

uebersetzungen.title: Certified translations — legally binding, personally handled.
uebersetzungen.p1: A certified translation bears the stamp, signature and certification statement of the authorised translator. It is valid in Germany before courts, public authorities, notaries and registry offices — and recognised abroad with an apostille.
uebersetzungen.p2: Common documents: birth, marriage and death certificates; diplomas and academic transcripts; powers of attorney; contracts; judgments and court filings; commercial register extracts; notarial deeds; estate documents.

locations.title: Available across NRW and Germany.
locations.p1: The practice is centred in North Rhine-Westphalia with short travel to the courts in Düsseldorf, Cologne, Mönchengladbach and Krefeld. On request I travel anywhere in Germany for longer engagements, business negotiations and plant visits.

differentiation.title: Why a person — and not a system.

items.title: Legal admissibility
items.body: Certified translations and court interpreting in Germany are tied to a personal oath (§ 189 GVG). What is spoken or written here will hold up before a judge.

items.title: Confidentiality
items.body: Professional duty of confidentiality, personal handling of sensitive material, no cloud services, no third-party disclosure.

items.title: On-site presence
items.body: Negotiations live on tone, pause and eye contact. Travel anywhere in Germany, including at short notice.

items.q: Who is allowed to interpret Lithuanian in German courts?
items.a: In German courts, only generally sworn interpreters may act in this capacity (§ 189 GVG). Daiva Jankus is generally sworn for the Lithuanian language by the Düsseldorf Higher Regional Court.

items.q: What does a sworn Lithuanian interpreter cost?
items.a: For court engagements the Judicial Remuneration Act (JVEG) applies. Private and business assignments are quoted on the basis of scope.

items.q: What is the difference between interpreting and translating?
items.a: Interpreting is the spoken transfer in real time; translation is its written counterpart. Each requires its own oath — Daiva Jankus is admitted for both.

items.q: Are certified translations recognised?
items.a: Yes — by authorities, courts and notaries throughout Germany. For use abroad, an apostille may be required.

items.q: Which documents are most often translated?
items.a: Birth, marriage and death certificates; diplomas and academic transcripts; powers of attorney; contracts; judgments and court filings; commercial register extracts.

items.q: How quickly can a short-notice booking be arranged?
items.a: Regular appointments with one to two weeks' lead time. Urgent matters (police, remand judge) at short notice.

items.q: Is confidentiality guaranteed?
items.a: Yes. Professional duty of confidentiality, personal handling, no cloud services, GDPR-compliant disposal after completion.

items.q: Which fee schedule applies in court (JVEG)?
items.a: For court engagements, fees and travel costs are settled under the Judicial Remuneration and Compensation Act (JVEG). A concrete statement is provided after the appointment.

crosslink.body: If your matter goes beyond interpreting — for example because a Lithuanian-speaking person can no longer make their own decisions — Mandate II comes into play.

closing.title: Request an appointment or a certified translation.

betreuung.metaTitle: Professional Legal Guardian Mönchengladbach | Lithuanian–German | Daiva Jankus
betreuung.metaDescription: Registered professional legal guardian under the BtOG in Mönchengladbach. Financial care, healthcare and residence matters. Lithuanian and German as working languages.

hero.title: Legal guardianship — personal, in language, binding.
hero.lead: Registered professional legal guardian under the Guardianship Organisation Act (BtOG), with documented qualification and a practice based in Mönchengladbach. Lithuanian and German as working languages — a rare combination that benefits family courts, social services and families.

scope.title: Three areas of responsibility.
scope.lede: The scope of duties is set by the guardianship court on a case-by-case basis. Common combinations are financial care, healthcare and residence — individually or together.

items.title: Financial care
items.desc: Administration of income, accounts and ongoing obligations. Tax matters, contractual affairs, asset protection in the interest of the person under care.

items.title: Healthcare
items.desc: Representation in medical decisions, communication with physicians and care facilities, exercise of patient rights and informed-consent conversations.
bullets: Liaison with hospital and care home

items.title: Residence & social
items.desc: Support in housing and care matters, residential contracts, applications for social benefits, contact with providers and authorities.

what.title: What legal guardianship means — and what it does not.
what.p1: Legal guardianship is ordered by the guardianship court for an adult who, due to a health-related or cognitive limitation, can no longer manage their affairs in whole or in part.
what.p2: Guardianship is limited to what is necessary and oriented toward the welfare and will of the person under care (§§ 1814 et seq. BGB). Scope and duration are set by the court in each case and reviewed regularly.
list: Guardianship is not legal incapacitation — that was abolished in 1992.
It does not replace the will of the person under care — it supports it.
It is not blanket — the scope is precisely tailored.
It is not permanent — it ends when it is no longer necessary.

vorschlag.title: Proposing Daiva Jankus as guardian.
vorschlag.p1: When a guardianship is ordered, those affected and close relatives have a right of proposal (§ 1816 BGB). The court is required to follow this proposal as a rule, unless suitability or conflict-of-interest concerns argue against it.
vorschlag.p2: Linguistic and cultural proximity to the affected person counts in the suitability assessment — an aspect in which Lithuanian-speaking families in Germany are systematically underserved.
steps: First contact — a brief description of the situation by phone or form.
In-person meeting — suitability check and clarification of the expected scope.
Proposal to the court — written proposal with proof of qualification and willingness to serve.
Appointment — the guardianship court reviews and appoints.

litauisch.title: Guardianship in the native language of the person under care.
litauisch.body: The language of childhood returns with age, often also with cognitive limitations. A Lithuanian-speaking person who has lived and worked in German for decades may suddenly only want — or be able — to understand Lithuanian in the care home. When treatment decisions, residential contracts or financial matters arise, the language used decides the dignity of the proceeding.

cases.title: Trust instead of translation
cases.body: When the guardian themselves speaks Lithuanian, the filter of a third party falls away. Personal conversations remain personal.

cases.title: Connection to family in Lithuania
cases.body: Relatives living in their home country can be involved in their own language — in decisions that touch the whole of a life.

cases.title: Suitability the court recognises
cases.body: Linguistic and cultural proximity are recognised as suitability criteria under § 1816 BGB — a qualification that must be formally documented.

differentiation.title: Why a person — and not a system.

items.title: Personal registration
items.body: Since 2023 professional guardians must be personally registered under the BtOG — with documented qualification, suitability and professional indemnity insurance.

items.title: Trusteeship over decisions about a life
items.body: Duty of confidentiality, personal record-keeping, liability under one's own name. Reporting duties to the guardianship court.

items.title: Attendance, not retrieval
items.body: Care conversations, authority appointments and clinical visits live on presence and judgement. Both call for being there.

items.q: What is legal guardianship in Germany?
items.a: Legal guardianship is ordered by the guardianship court for adults who can no longer manage their affairs in whole or in part (§§ 1814 et seq. BGB).

items.q: What is a professional guardian under the BtOG?
items.a: Legal guardians acting in a professional capacity with documented qualification, registered with the competent authority under the Guardianship Organisation Act (BtOG). Requirements: personal suitability, qualification certificate, professional indemnity insurance.

items.q: In which areas does Daiva Jankus act?
items.a: Financial care, healthcare and residence matters, as well as dealings with authorities and housing — depending on the court order.

items.q: Can I propose Daiva Jankus as guardian?
items.a: Yes. Those affected or their relatives can propose a particular person (§ 1816 BGB). The court is required to follow this proposal as a rule.

items.q: Who bears the cost of guardianship?
items.a: For persons of limited means, the state treasury pays the flat-rate compensation under the VBVG. For those with means, it is paid from the assets of the person under care.

items.q: How is a guardianship ended or amended?
items.a: Guardianship ends as soon as it is no longer necessary. The court reviews regularly (at the latest after seven years) and can extend or restrict the scope.

items.q: What duties does a professional guardian have?
items.a: Personal exercise of the scope of duties, reporting to the court, annual financial accounting, respect for the right to self-determination of the person under care.

items.q: How does guardianship differ from a power of attorney?
items.a: A precautionary power of attorney is one you grant yourself in advance for the case of legal incapacity. A guardianship is ordered by the court when no effective power of attorney exists or it is not sufficient.

crosslink.body: If your matter concerns language mediation in courts or with authorities — before or alongside a guardianship — Mandate I comes into play.

ueber.metaDescription: Daiva Jankus — sworn Lithuanian–German interpreter and registered professional legal guardian. Three decades between two languages and two responsibilities.

hero.title: Three decades between two languages — and two responsibilities.
hero.lead: Born in Lithuania, trained in Vilnius, working in Germany since 1995. Generally sworn interpreter and authorised translator for Lithuanian–German (OLG Düsseldorf), and since 2023 a registered professional legal guardian under the BtOG. Practice based in Mönchengladbach, active in NRW and across Germany.

bio.title: Career path.
bio.p1: Raised between the two languages that today form the foundation of my work. Studies in Germanic studies and translation studies in Vilnius, followed by postgraduate study in Germany. Settled in North Rhine-Westphalia in 1995.
bio.p2: General oath as interpreter and authorisation as translator by the Düsseldorf Higher Regional Court. Over three decades of engagements before courts, notaries, in business negotiations and in personal representation.
bio.p3: In 2023, registered as a professional guardian under the Guardianship Organisation Act (BtOG) with documented qualification. This extended the practice to include personal representation of adults — a responsibility connected to the previous practice in language, ethics and law.
bio.p4: Both practices come together especially where Lithuanian-speaking people in Germany are concerned — older generations in care facilities, families with relatives in Lithuania, court proceedings in which language and self-determination are inseparable.

items.title: Settled in Germany
items.body: Beginning of freelance practice as interpreter and translator in North Rhine-Westphalia.

items.title: General oath
items.body: Sworn as interpreter and authorised as translator for the Lithuanian language by the Düsseldorf Higher Regional Court.

items.title: Three decades of practice
items.body: Over a thousand engagements before courts, notaries, authorities and in business negotiations. Accompanying translation of official and private documents.

items.title: Registered as professional guardian
items.body: Documented qualification and registration under the BtOG with the competent authority. Extension of practice to legal guardianship.

items.title: Two mandates, one location
items.body: Practice in Mönchengladbach. Active across NRW and Germany, in both mandates — in German and Lithuanian.
